item.title: Introduction
item.description: Each method is discussed in the following structure: What?: What’s the idea of the method? What does it do? Why?: Why do we need it?

item.title: Data
item.description: Notations: $m$: #samples $n$: #features $X=[\mathbf{x}_1,\cdots,\mathbf{x}_m]^T$: input matrix of shape $(m,n)$ $\mathbf{y}=[y_1,\cdots,y_{m}]^T$: output vector of length $m$ Cleaning Remove unwanted observations: e.

item.title: Unsupervised Learning
item.description: Clustering Clustering groups similar samples together without any prior knowledge of the clusters. K-Means Idea: Hard clustering (clustering with deterministic results)

item.title: Supervised Learning
item.description: Linear Models Naive Bayes What?: Naive Bayes is the simplest generative, parametric classifier directly using Bayes’ Theorem. Why?: It solves problems in scenarios where we need quick and accurate predictions.

item.title: Optimization
item.description: Loss Loss is a measure of difference between predicted output $\hat{y}_i$ and actual output $y_i$. Unreduced loss: Reduced loss: $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{y},\mathbf{\hat{y}})=\begin{cases} \text{sum}(\mathcal{L})=\sum_{i=1}^{m}l_i\\ \text{mean}(\mathcal{L})=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}l_i \end{cases}$ (can impose sample weights as well) Regression L1 (Absolute Error) Pros:

item.title: Evaluation
item.description: Metrics Classification Confusion Matrix source Metric Def Formula Type I Error reject $H_0$ when true (predict P when N) $P(FP)$ Type II Error accept $H_0$ when false (predict N when P) $P(FN)$ Accuracy % of correct predictions $P(TP)+P(TN)$ Precision % of actual Ps among predicted Ps $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ Recall/Sensitivity % of predicted Ps among actual Ps $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ Specificity % of predicted Ns among actual Ns $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ F1-score Balance tradeoff between precision & recall $2\frac{\text{prec}*\text{rec}}{\text{prec}+\text{rec}}$ F$\boldsymbol{\beta}$-score Place importance on recall $\beta$ times of precision $(1+\beta^2)\frac{\text{prec}*\text{rec}}{(\beta^2*\text{prec})+\text{rec}}$ ROC & AUC source TPR (True Positive Rate): = Recall, $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ FPR (False Positive Rate): = 1 – Specificity, $\frac{FP}{TN+FP}$ Classification threshold: anything above this defined value is classified as 1, $P(y=1)$ (default 0.

item.title: Concepts
item.description: Universal Approximation Theorem: A neural network with 1 hidden layer can approximate any continuous function for inputs within a specific range.

item.title: Layer
item.description: A layer is fundamentally a function that transforms input $X$ into output $Y$. This page assumes no activation. Basics Linear $$ Y=XW^T+\textbf{b} $$

item.title: Activation
item.description: An activation function adds nonlinearity to the output of a layer (linear in most cases) to enhance complexity. ReLU and Softmax are SOTA.

item.title: Toolbox
item.description: This page is a collection of tools that are widely used in model construction and experimentation, including Regularization Optimization Activation Regularization Regularization reduces overfitting by adding our prior belief onto loss minimization (i.
